title: Unraveling the Bott spiral
authors: Arun Debray, Cameron Krulewski, Luuk Stehouwer
abstract: We construct and compute a homotopy-theoretic model for the Bott spiral of symmetry-protected topological phases (SPTs) studied by Queiroz--Khalaf--Stern. We model free and interacting fermionic SPTs using K-theory and reflection-positive invertible field theories (IFTs), resp., and define a twisted generalization of the Atiyah--Bott--Shapiro orientation to produce a free-to-interacting map. We also define and compute spiral maps of IFTs to model dimensional reduction in this context, answering a question of Hason--Komargodski--Thorngren. Our analysis highlights two general aspects of homotopical free-to-interacting maps. First, IFTs are more sensitive than K-theory is to the input symmetry data; in particular, the specification of an Altland--Zirnbauer class is insufficient information to define symmetry type for an IFT. Second, the remnant of Bott periodicity on the interacting side relies on an isomorphism of two extraspecial groups of order 32. Our computations use a novel 4-periodic description of a sector of the twisted ko-homology of elementary abelian 2-groups.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous mathematical framework to understand how the periodic classification of topological phases (the Bott clock) transforms into an expanding spiral when particle interactions are introduced. It successfully links free fermion K-theory to interacting field theories, explaining the exponential growth of topological phases in higher dimensions.
main_problem: The paper aims to construct a homotopy-theoretic model to compare the classification of free fermion symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phases with their interacting counterparts, specifically addressing the 'Bott spiral' where interacting classifications grow exponentially with dimension.
main_result: The authors define a 'free-to-interacting' map using a twisted generalization of the Atiyah-Bott-Shapiro orientation and show that the interacting classification 'unravels' into a spiral where the group order increases by a factor of 16 every eight dimensions.
method: The study employs advanced algebraic topology, including K-theory, Anderson duality, cobordism theory, and the construction of twisted spin structures and spectra maps.
model_or_system: The system consists of fermionic SPT phases within the Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry classes, modeled using K-theory for free fermions and reflection-positive invertible field theories (IFTs) for interacting systems.
key_observables: Topological invariants, the order of the long summand in the IFT groups, and the kernel/cokernel of the free-to-interacting map.
important_parameters: Symmetry indices (l, k), dimension (d), and the Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry classes.
main_assumptions: The paper assumes the Freed-Hopkins ansatz, where the low-energy limit of an SPT is modeled by an invertible field theory.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a mathematical model of the Bott spiral, illustrating the growth of the group Z/2^N and the relationship between Clifford algebras and dimensional reduction maps.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the symmetry ambiguity problem, proposes a unified framework using fermionic groups, constructs the free-to-interacting map via twisted ABS orientation, defines spiral maps for dimensional reduction, and validates the results against known interacting group orders.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The Bott spiral starting with 1d class BDI′: our mathematical model for [QKS16, Figure 1(a)], which displays the groups of interacting SPTs admitting free fermion realizations in a specified symmetry class and indicates dimensional reduction maps between them. Our model for an interacting SPT class is a defor- mation class of invertible field theory (IFT; see Ansatz 2.11), and our models for dimensional reduction are the spiral maps spϕ ℓ,k and spψ ℓ,k defined in Definition 4.51 and calculated in Theorems 6.16 and 6.24. In each entry of the spiral, Z/2N is the group of IFTs in the image of a map from KO-theory, calculated in Proposition 5.55 and Theorem 6.15. The bracket below the...
Figure 2. The Bott spiral starting with 1d class CII: our mathematical model for [QKS16, Figure 1(b)]. All notation is as in Figure 1.

Figure 3. This figure shows the sequence of interacting SPT phases in the complex version of the Bott spiral, starting with the d = 1 class AIII insulator, which is predicted to generate the group ℧2 Pinc ∼= Z/4 in the top right. The leftmost column indicates the Altland–Zirnbauer class of the SPT at each step. The middle column encodes spectra of the form MTSpinc ∧MEℓ,k and the maps between them. The last column indicates the IZ-cohomology of the middle column; i.e. the groups of (deformation classes of) invertible field theories that provide our model for the groups of interacting SPTs. The spatial dimension of the SPT increases as the rightmost column is read downward; see Remark 4.56....

Figure 10. Left: the A(1)-module N2, the unique non-free summand of Σ−2 eH∗(BA4; Z/2). Right: Ext(N2), the E2-page of the Adams spectral sequence for a summand of ko ∧Σ−2BA4. All differentials and extension questions are trivial. We discuss N2 and its Adams spectral sequence in Example 5.34.

Figure 12. Left: the A(1)-module N0 := Σ−1H∗((BZ/2)1−σ; Z/2). Right: Ext(N0), the E2-page of the Adams spectral sequence for Σ−1ko ∧(BZ/2)1−σ. All differentials and extension questions are trivial. See Example 5.40 for more information.
